notes.note:
Bought as untested from Drew Sharp down by Atlanta.  Said dad had 3 of these.  Included ProFile hard drive for an additional $100.  Came with Owner’s Guide, keyboard, and Printer.  

RIFA cap blew while adjusting vertical hold. Replaced RIFA caps with X2 safety caps, replaced all electrolytic in the PSU and video board.  Second memory board had memory error preventing boot up, removed. Widget internal hard drive was working and had Lisa OS 3.1 installed.  Floppy was seized and needed full restoration.  Was able to get it allowing floppies to be inserted and ejected manually.   ProFile drive had blown RIFA and blown fuse.  Replacing all RIFAs and electrolytic. Keyboard not functional, so replacing the foam and tape.  Retrobrighted all panels

Was able to get FloppyEmu to boot MacWorks XL by updating the FloppyEmu firmware.  Will try to install LOS and MacWorks XL onto the Parallel Hard Drive Emulator (Cameo/Aphid device from ArcaneBytes).
Hoping to mount it inside, with microUSB port in expansion slot on the back, along with external Floppy Port in another for plugging in FloppyEmu.  

Was able to get it running stable with HD emulator.  Has LOS3.1 and Macworks XL installed.  2nd Memory card showed errors in the ICs at C7 and C16.  Socketed the spots and am waiting on some replacement chips to see if the issue resolves.  

Repaired broken legs on Caps Lock key by removing original material and replacing the legs with a 3d printed part.  Much better connection and angle on the key now.  

Drew said he had found the mouse.  Trying to see if I can get it to complete the original set.
Replaced foam and foil in keyboard and all keys now work.

Swapped out memory chip at C16 that reported bad with a new one and the machine now boots without a memory error.  Also, odd crashes in MacWorks seem to have gone away.  Maybe it needs 1MB RAM.  I socketed another memory chip at C7 because it originally reported as bad, but after socketing the chip and re-inserting the reported bad chip, it did not error during the memory checks on boot up.  Still socketed in case it goes bad.  Have 3 spare chips if needed.

Swapped out the Cameo/Aphid for an ESProFile because it can be powered internally from the Widget power connector.  I now have 4 extra working ESProfiles, and 5 boards that simply need ESP32 dev boards flashed & installed.
Set up the Lisa to test some ESProfiles for a guy who wanted to buy some.  The image is very shaky.  May need to adjust the pots on the video board.

devices.additionalName: Recapped Logic / Analog, BlueSCSI - low sound
devices.additionalInfo:
Originally booted with no sound from speakers or headphone jack. Looked into possible grounding issue, but it appears to be specific to this machines logic board.  When I swap in a known good logic board sound works.  

I replaced the sound chip and there was some trace damage so I've added some bodge wires. Finally found the issue when recapping the logic board.  One of the traces from the sound chip to the cap was broken.  Fixed with bodge wires and sound now works. 

Sound is still pretty quiet, and I suspect maybe the grounding from the logic board to the case isn’t good due to some rust on the case shielding. 

Additionally, one of the edge connector pins was broken off so I had to fix with the broken pin and a bodge wire.  I take extra care when inserting and removing the logic board. 

Analog board was recapped on 2022-07-22. Logic board on 2025-08-09.

notes.note:
Bought from Facebook Marketplace as “brand new”. Corrosion on ADB and serial ports, and after opening, as expected the battery had exploded, the battery holder, and crystal were all gone from the board and there was significant corrosion to many chips on the board. The RF shield on the back plate was half gone and had been bent.  CRT flickers a bit when turned on, but seems to work well, but needs adjustments to geometry.  Machine worked when I put in working board (with repaired pin) from other Color Classic that now has the mystic board in. Came with keyboard, mouse, and getting started guide, as well as a set of floppies. Was able to install 7.1 from the floppies to external BlueSCSI as a test. Hard Drive sounds bad and isn’t recognized.

Disassembled for retrobright and there was rust on shield inside case.  Also, seems to be significant cap leakage around a couple of caps.  Will order recap kit and replace all ASAP.

Recapped analog board.  Re-repaired the logic board edge connector and seems to work well with this case / analog board.  Need to adjust the geometry. Also, appears to have no sound, so need to investigate. Didn’t seem to be grounding issue, as found in Adrians Digital Basement.  Also, no sound from headphones, so may be dead sound chip.  Maybe salvage from battery bombed board?

Tried CC logic board in the Performa 550 (since the 550 logic board seems to have an issue).  No sound, but booted otherwise just fine.  Sound issue appears to be logic board related.  Might look to replace the sound chip with one from the battery bombed board.

Replaced sound chip with one from battery bombed CC.  Had a few pads lift and had to run a couple of bodge wires.  Unfortunately, no change.  Still boots with no sound from speakers or headphone jack.  Will likely have to dig in to see if there’s a damaged trace somewhere else on the board.

Recapped the logic board and found that one of the traces from the sound chip to the nearby capacitor had been broken. Repaired that trace with a bad wire, but still have no sound from the speakers or the headphone jack. Then noticed one of the grounding points below logic board was bent.  Bent it back and I get sound again.  

Edge connector wasn’t making contact so I soldered a bodge wire from it to the correct pin.

devices.additionalInfo:
Purchase from guy in Elizabeth neighborhood in Charlotte.  

Wasn't sure if it was a 512k upgrade or 128, but the Serial Number showed it as a 128k, so I was hoping it was.  The logic board is indeed a 128k, but it has some mods done (all memory chips have been socketed and a new socket and chip was piggybacked onto another with some resistors.  Basically making it a 128k with 512k memory (aka FatMac).

Analog board was having issues, so I took it from my Mac Plus that was an upgraded 512k.  

Floppy drive works.

notes.note:
Inspected boards and they looked good.  There was some corrosion inside the battery bay, but I cleaned it and it looks like it didn't spread to analog board.  

When attempting to power on, the analog board would click.  Maybe not enough voltage to get it to boot.  When I put the logic board into the Mac 512k, it booted fine with nice loud startup chime, and I used FloppyEMU to load System 3.2.  It showed 512k memory. Going to get caps for Analog board and hope it fixes the issue.  

Also considering getting KayKoba's FatMac Switcher that supposedly lets you switch between 128k and 512k.
Recapped analog and logic boards and the fast ticking sound remains.  Something else on the analog board must be bad.

Swapped in analog board from the Mac Plus (upgraded 512k).machine now boots properly.  Adjusted voltage and geometry and crt looks good. 

The floppy drive was seized up in a state where it thought a disk was in. Caused the eject motor to run and draw too much power which caused the floppyemu to reboot repeatedly

Cleaned hardened grease on floppy and re-lubricated things. Cleaned read-write head with isopropyl alcohol.  Seems to read and write disks.  Made system 3.2 floppy with HD20 support.

Reconditioned it, replaced the eject gear (still in tact, but cracked when I put pressure on the teeth), and put dioxit in eject location switch.

notes.note:

Powered on and got a boot chime, but not image.  Removed logic board (requires prying loose because of two additional connectors - one for SCSI port and another for connecting to external device.  Additionally, the analog board had J-clips attaching to the extra box, as well as two going to a fan over the high voltage component on the analog board.  Also, looks like maybe the CRT glass nipple is broken.  Keyboard worked properly when connected to older Mac Plus.Plans are to recap the analog board (harder with apparent modifications to logic board), try with different CRT tube, and see if I get an image. 

CRT was fine, but screen has significant burn in.  Swapped analog board, CRT, and floppy with 512k (upgraded to plus).  Was able to get it to boot from BlueSCSI with software for 030 accelerator. Unfortunately, I left it running for a few hours so see how it would do and the analog board seems to have had a component failure.  The screen showed diagonal lines.  Swapped back in analog board originally from this machine after being recapped and voltages seem good.  Occasionally the screen will pulse. 
 
After fixing the diagonal line issue (U2 replacement solved it), I have again put the CRT and analog board from the Stock Plus from eBay, as it was the cleanest board, no burns on J1 connector, no burn in, etc.  Seems to be working well now.

notes.note:
Purchased off FB Marketplace.  Came with Printer, 2 ADB keyboards, 2 serial mice, a USB Zip 250 drive, an Apple II disk controller, an unidentified RAM card, an Apple printer, and a bunch of software and manuals. 

Photos of it on showed it with a vertical jailbar pattern.  Trying to disassemble the case screws in the handle area were filled with packed dirt.  Once I got it apart, the machine actually looked very good inside.  The PRAM battery hadn't leaked, the caps looked OK.  I brushed away the dust and dirt.  Will give it a thorough cleaning and replace caps, but wanted to test functionality first.  The CRT had severe burn-in, and I wasn't sure about the power supply and analog board, so I plugged it into the SE/30 reloaded machine that has a logic board problem.  Surprisingly, it booted right up.  

Attached a BlueSCSI and it booted into AUX fine.  No audio from speaker, but might need to double check the connections.  Also could be a bad Sony chip.  

The woman I bought from said her late husband was responsible for setting up the Charlotte Mac Users group back in the 90s.  And arranged to have Steve Wozniak come speak at an event when they first started.

No audio on startup.  Also, hangs when trying to play a sound.  Read on forums it could be a problem with caps.  I recapped it and issue persists.  The sound chips had a lot of corrosion on legs.  I removed them and added sockets.  Cleaned the legs as best I could and reinstalled, same issue.  I suspect sound chips are bad.  May try pulling from my SE/30 reloaded board, since it won't boot, but unfortunately, I soldered them onto the board, so I'd need to de-solder them.

Reading a 68kmla forum post about sound issues resulting from connections to/from the UE10 chip, I tested them out.  Sure enough, I found a break between the pad for pin 11 and the via that would take it to the GLUE chip.  Soldered on a bodge wire and it started up with a nice loud bong.  No longer hangs on playing certain sounds!

Cleaned, lubricated the floppy drive.  Replaced auto eject gear. Tested and working, although the eject seems to fall short of fully ejecting the disk, which means it tries to pul the disk back in, but fails, causing the disk to mistakenly try to read a half inserted disk.  Using the manual eject hole resolves, but not ideal.  May need to pull it out and reassemble...
Sold to neonfire on TinkerDifferent forums

Bought for $10 off Facebook Marketplace of $10, but gave him $20 since he threw in an original StyleWriter, a keyboard and mouse. I then replaced the video ram and got a clear gray picture, but it was still too short vertically.  After getting the RAM just right, I got it to boot, so SCSI and ADB appear to work, but picture is still distorted from being too short.

Purchased from church of FB Marketplace. Drove to GA (South of Savannah) to pickup.  Had jail bar pattern in front of question mark floppy. Some damage from leaking caps.  Component at Q3 fell off board while cleaning.  Will need to replace.  

After recap, still have some artifacts on screen and sometimes jail pattern.  Also doesn’t seem to recognize the external SCSI with BlueSCSI. Corrosion on SCSI chip.  Tried to clean off, but could be pin rot.  Tried with original memory, as well as new 4 x 1MB SIMMs.  Same result with graphics artifacts and jail pattern.
Tried replacing video RAM with socketed chips, same jailbars.  Forum on ml68k indicates could be UE8 chip.  I removed, and realized it had some pins rotted out.  Waiting to replace.

Replaced UE8 chip and video worked properly again.  SCSI still did not, found SCSI chip had issues.  When replacing SCSI chip, there were damaged / ripped pads.  Tried to bodge wire fix them, but cannot get machine to boot.
Tried to transplant to SE/30 reloaded board, but get no boot chime.

Received email from Colin that the reloaded boards from the group buy have bad diodes installed on the bottom of the board.  Probably not the only issue, but maybe one of my issues with not getting a boot chime?
Replaced diodes and there was no difference. Still no boot chime and no video.  Also replaced diodes in second reloaded board.

Confirmed that the CPU, video ROM, ROM SIMM, UE6, UE7, UG6, UG7, and UI6 are all good by putting them in socketed SE/30 board that boots.  With known good chips from the other socketed board, the machine still does not boot chime or show video.

Started looking at the surface mount chips and realized I had some pins that were not making contact on UD12 and UE12. Removed and resoldered, confirming every leg had connection to board.  Still no chime or video.  

Then looked at UH7 and found two pins that didn't have connections.  One to the GLU chip and one to a via I didn't trace.  Fixed those and I got a gray screen, but no mouse!  The ADB chip is also surface mount, so I took a look at that, and sure enough, I had one pin there that did not have continuity to the board.  With that pin soldered properly, I was able to get a boot chime, a pointer, and it would boot Mac OS 7.5, but froze on boot.  After adding more memory, it would boot into 7.5, but looked frozen.  So I threw 6.0.8 on a BlueSCSI and booted again.  Again, it appeared to freeze after startup.  Then I realized that the mouse was simply not moving.  Weird ADB behavior.  When the mouse is plugged into the keyboard, I get no mouse movement. When the mouse is plugged into the second ADB port on the logic board, it moves, but very slowly and jerky (like it is one frame of movement for every random couple of seconds.  Also, typing results in multiple duplicated key presses, and missed key presses.  Definitely need to investigate the ADB issues, but at least it boots.  

Might be best to remove and resolder the ADB chip.

OK, I re-inspected the ADB chip and there was another leg not connected.  Reconnected and the mouse was working normally.  Swapping cases with another SE/30 to have this one be my top one with the reloaded board, 20MB RAM, BlueSCSI accessible from the expansion port.
Added Big Mess o’ Wires Rominator II to it.  Used ResEdit to modify 7.5.3 system files to allow it to boot with new ROM.

Replaced Power Supply with a new recreation from here: https://github.com/tt-design-studio/psu-mac-se

Purchased with Stylewriter II printer, keyboard, and 3rd party ADB mouse. Originally wouldn’t power on, but let analog board sit plugged in, removed the battery, and cleaned the logic board. The machine powered on, screen looks good, original hard drive containing Mac OS System 7.1P6 booted up fine. 

Original hard drive still works.  Pulled data onto backup BlueSCSI image.  Need to test floppy and CD-ROM drive.
Tried to power it on and I can hear the PSU power up, but when I press the soft power button on the keyboard, there is no boot chime.  Also, there is no picture on the CRT.  HD spins up (loudly).
Put De-Oxit on ROM sockets and reinserted. Recapped the logic board and it still has no boot chime.
I tried the 550 logic board in the color classic and got the same behavior - no boot chime, no picture, so problem seems to be with the logic board.

Removed L1 and L2 and cleaned underneath - traces look good.  Machine still refuses to output image or produce boot sound.  It does power on from soft power key from the keyboard.  Going to run the logic board through the dishwasher to try to do a thorough cleaning.  Also, should note that the high and low rom sockets seem to have more holes than the ROM chips have pins.  May need to make sure I have it offset properly.

Removed sound chip and cleaned underneath but it looked good.  Tried with different RAM, then no RAM, it has 4MB on-board, still no change. 

Spoke to Thomas Andrews of Amiga of Rochester and he believed it could be failed ROM, and that I could possibly try ROM from a color classic just to see if it would power on.  No luck when I used spare CC ROMs.

notes.note: Bought thinking it was genuine Apple II.  Have had issues with it booting, thinking maybe bad RAM chips.  When boots, have problems loading apps from disk drive.  Also have problems loading from cassette emulator.

Decided to try to get it working.  Removed all memory chips and reseated after hitting sockets with DeOxit.  It would alternate between gibberish on screen, an all white screen, or a white screen with a few colored blocks.  

I swapped the CPU with the one from the IIe and I was able to get it to boot to a screen filled with Apple logo characters.  Maybe a bad ROM?   Ordered a new CPU from Amazon and will see if the machine still boots to Apple characters filling the screen.

Unfortunately, I fried the floppy controller while troubleshooting.  I was able to get it to boot sometimes by replacing the first row of RAM chips with new ones.  Unfortunately, I was still not able to get software to load from the cassette jack.  I did replace the sockets as I found one that seemed to have damage.

After putting the case back on, I noticed the behavior changed if I pressed on the D8 ROM chip.  I removed it and re-inserted it and one of the legs ripped off, unfortunately very close the the chip, so I don't think I can solder it back on.   Will order the ROMx from ReactMicro and see if I can get a stable machine.

Got with very well kept copies of Oregon Trail, Castle Wolfenstein, Choplifter, and Dig Dug. And original power supply. Keyboard was very hard to press keys.  Removed rubber mats underneath and adjusted pins - now typing is much better.

JCM-1 took the logic board and found the corrupt video was caused by a bad LS283 at E11.  Also had issue using his wDrive and discovered the System ROM was bad, so installed a new on.  

Video output is now working correctly, but now the machine's PSU will enter a short state that causes is to click rapidly after being used for a bit.  Replacing with a new one from ReactiveMicro.

notes.note:
One of the monitors has a tear in the anti-glare screen, so I need to open it up and remove it.  The other is just very dirty, so I want to open it up and clean the screen and the glass CRT behind it.  

The estimated value for this is ~$500-600 for the Apple III, $50 each for the monitors, and $200 for the ProFile drive.

OK, so I let the ProFile run a bit longer and the flashing red light appears to just be it running its diagnostics. It takes a really long time, but eventually it got a solid red "Ready" light! Without the interface card, I might have to see if I can use this with my Lisa.

Picked up on FB Marketplace from a guy in Belmont, NC.  Said he was on a job and paid to clean out an old lawyer's storage.  Found the Apple ///, two Apple Monitor ///s, a ProFile 5MB drive, and external floppy.  The Apple /// had a Universal Parallel Interface card, but unfortunately no ProFile interface card.  
The ProFile would spin up, but the red "Ready" light would never become solid.  It would blink repeatedly.  The Apple /// would start up, but showed gibberish on the screen.  When I Apple, Command Reset, it appears the screen will change when I type, so maybe it is booting, but the video circuit is bad?
Bought in a bundle for $400.  Break down costs as $200 for the Apple ///, $100 for the ProFile drive, $20 for the Disk ///, and $40 each for the two Apple Monitor ///s.
Found markings on bottom of PSU I think it reads “BAD 10-20-08 From DAH”

Hoping to get it working with a recapped power supply, and maybe a new video rom.  Also, if I get it working, I'd like to get color video out using this: https://jcm-1.com/product/universal-video-adapter-for-apple3/ and maybe a CGA to VGA adapter to be able to use my LCD.

So, the machine is definitely booting - I was able to create an ADTPro SOS disk on the Apple IIc+ and boot with the disk in the Apple III. I can see a corrupted ADT screen and even interact with it.  It seems a bit different than the ProDOS one, so I'm not sure what the options are.  Hoping the Video ROM is bad and replacing it will correct the display issues I'm seeing.  See 2024-11-20 picture for how the ADT screen looks.

Had the suggestion to swap the video RAM chips (2114) at E4 and E5 to see if the pattern changed (indicating a bad SRAM chip), but it made no difference.  

Going to see if I can trace out the connections from the video controller ROM and see if the chip is connected where it should be.

Checked all the pins on the Video controller ROM except 3,9,12, and 24 as I couldn't find where those should go in the schematics.  12 and 24 seem to not be connected to anything. Pin 3 I couldn't read on the schematic, and Pin 9 (PGZ or PG2) I couldn't find either.

All the traces I could see in the schematic tested good.  While I wait for the video controller ROM, I ordered new ICs for most of the chips in the video circuitry. See picture with highlighted items in green for the ones ordered.

replaced all the 74 series ICs in the video schematics and replaced all with new parts.  No change in behavior. replaced B10, D4, E2,E3,E8,F2,F3,F4,F8,G2,G3,H2,H3,H4,H7,H8,H10,J3,K8,K9,L8.  Put back originals for J3 and F4 because they were not LS series chips.  

Also noticed that the board has H8 labeled as LS04 when the schematics show LS14.
Swapped the video controller ROM with one from Joe's Computer Museum, and no difference in video output. :(  Going to have to dig into what is wrong.

Recapped PSU and problem persists (I didn't think that the problem was related to the PSU, but wanted to rule it out). Joe from JCM suggested maybe timing related and to look at counters  74LS161 chips at G11, G12.  I swapped the chips and got no difference in behavior.  So I'll probable have to dig into the timing circuitry...
Started looking into the timing circuit.  Replaced B11, B13, G10.  I rotated 9334 chips at H6, L12, L13.  Rotated LS161 chips at F10, F11, G11, G12.  Swapped S74 chips at D11 and D5.

Apple /// users on Facebook suggested maybe the CPU could be the source of the timing issue, so I ordered another from ReActiveMicro.  Unfortunately, no change in behavior.  Going to send board and components to Joe from JCM and he's offered to take a look into it.
Mailed logic board to Joes Computer Museum for him to see if he can get it sorted out.

JCM-1 seems to have found the issue.  Looks like it turned out to be a faulty LS283 at E11.  He was able to swap it out and get a good video, but the machine would freeze after 30s.  The freezing turned out to be the wDrive he was using.  He's going go over the board to make sure there are no other issues, but it seems like I may get this machine working!
Seems like the issue with the wDrive may have been due to the system ROM.  JCM-1 is going to put a new ROM in it.  May also have him upgrade the RAM while he has it.

Got logic board back from JCM-1 and it is now working and spitting out good video.  However, after using the machine for a little bit, the PSU seems to warm up and short out, entering a state where it repeatedly clicks.   Powering off and back on does the same, unless you let it stay powered off for a bit and cool off.  I ordered a ReactiveMicro replacement PSU kit.

notes.note: Initially powered on when I tested, but I noted a smoky smell and immediately shut it off.  I cleaned the board and tested again and it worked, but with smoky smell coming from PSU area.  So I tested with another PSU and it worked but the smell remained.   Unfortunately it shut off and wouldn’t power back on.  I researched and other owners had issues with C39 and C40.  I removed and tested.  Both seem good at ~22uF.  Didn’t notice damage to any chips on the board.  No cap leakage.  Board looks pristine.

Found the source of the smell. The 10uF 16v tantalum at C34 had failed. It  tested filly open. Replaced it with one removed from the 6300 that turned out to be good, but machine still won’t power on.  Something else cooked when it failed open?
Just tried again and it boots and runs.

notes.note: Seller claimed working. Battery bombed.  Damage seemed limited to SIM slot 1, but damaged board trying to desolder the RAM slot.  Removed RAM (cleaned up and soldered broken pin on one hit by battery juice), VRAM, and CPU.  Case is scratched, but otherwise not bad.
Removed the SIMM socket. Ordered a replacement from Mouser. I did try to boot it up, and I get a pop from the speaker when powered on and the LED comes on, but no boot chime.

Replaced SIMM slot, repairs damaged traces on underside of board.  Was still not able to get a boot chime. 

I realized the board was missing some chips that were there on my other Q650 / Q800 board. U18 and U22 were removed from the board. As was RP7. So I pulled the chips from another battery bombed board and was able to get a boot chime, followed by a sad Mac. Some of the memory chips near the battery looked a bit suspect, so I removed one of the banks of memory chips, U44, u45, U49, U50, U53, U54, U60, and U61. I cleaned and tested the traces I could see and they seemed good. And I got a boot chime and no sad Mac. I also got video out, but it was corrupt.

Realized U3 was also missing. Replaced with one from another board but video is still corrupt.  Either VRAM installed, switching to thousands of colors the corruption is basically gone.  

Also, I get sad Mac chimes when I have SIMMs in slots 2 or 4.

Tried adding back other ram chips from the other 650 boards to the empty spots and the sad Mac chimes returned, so I removed them.  

Someone on tinkerDifferent formlums suggested the d28 or d29 was stuck high. I used a 475 schema to try to track PD28 and PD29 from the U8 chip to the vram simm slots.  They went through RC4, and also connected to U20.  All the connections seem good. So I tried replacing U8.  After replacing the problem remains. 

May want to try replacing U20 next.

Verified a bunch of traces around D28/29 (and PD28/29, BDb28/29, and BDa28/29). Didn't find any broken traces.

Also swapped the CPU and U20 and the problem persists.

Found the problem.  I noticed there was a short between the 5v line and the BDb29 line at the RP6 resistor pack.  I followed the traces and realized the BDb29 line was one I had repaired with a bodge wire around the damaged SIMMs. Turns out the damage must have shorted the traces for the BDb29 line at pin 60 and the 5v line at pin 59.  

After cutting the traces to isolate the 5v line from the data line, the video was fixed.   Also, the simm slots 2 and 4 now work.  Tested with SIMMs in all 4 slots now work.

Pushing down to ensure the key was connected to the switch, and the pole guide was clipped into the board again.

Picked up in shop haul.  Was filthy and had bare red and black wires sticking out the back where the barrel jack should be.  Cleaned the case, then took it apart to clean and inspect the insides.  There was a good amount of dirt.  

The barrel jack port was got and replaced with wire, and the red wire had broken loose.  I used a barrel jack connector from the Mac Portable, modified the pins to the board, and was able to get it installed. With the new barrel jack, and a 12v power supply, I was able to get it to power on, and even be seen on SCSI Probe on a Macintosh, but the drive wouldn't spin.  there is a switch that detects when the cover is closed, and when I tested it with a multimeter, it seemed to not be working.  I ordered a possible replacement, but opened up the existing one to see how it works.  I found a slider with a copper jumper that slides between the two sets of pins.  That jumper and the pins were blackened.  So I cleaned them, re-attached the side cover, and it was now working.  However, the unit will still not spin up.  I suspect there is another sensor to determine if a CD is inserted that may also be not working.

I was able to put some aluminum foil in the compartment that allows for the connection to happen and the camera powers on! Disassembled the machine, removed all cards except video card, removed both drives. Was able to get it to power up and show text on CRT.

Removed the CRT and then tried to put in CRT from a Max Plus I had that had a bad psu or analog board.  Unfortunately the pins on the neck of the plus crt wouldn’t allow the neck board from the 5155 to insert.  I took the Plus crt, put it in the SE 1/40 and put the SE CRT into this machine.  Attached and adjusted the yoke and the image looks pretty good.  Will need to load software to ensure it is level and the right size.  Also removed the broken pieces of the handle and may try to create a new one with a bar of aluminum, bent and cut.

devices.additionalInfo:
Full set including computer, monitor, keyboard, mouse, and printer.  

Recapped power supply in the monitor (serves as power supply for both monitor and computer). Also recapped keyboard.  Fully working with original hard drive and boots to a picker-style menu that allows you to launch DOS, individual programs, IBM's helper app, or Works.  Also has Windows 3.0 installed and can launch from DOS.

notes.note: Computer trips the GFCI when I try to power it on.  Post say it may be due to bad caps, so will open it up and remove some of the caps.

Fully disassembled the CRT. I thought I found leaking on some capacitors in the power board, but it looks like it was just old glue that had disintegrated. However, I did replace the three 2200 µF capacitors at C405, C401, and C308. The capacitor at C401 was a 35V cap. The other two were 25V, with C308 being the short cap.  Unfortunately, all caps tested at the right capacitance.

Recapped the PSU and the large caps on the analog board.  And it now boots!   I get a CMOS error and a keyboard error. The cmos error is because I need to be able to go into the bios and set settings new that the cmos battery is replaced.  But the keyboard error means I can’t do that.

Found that the IBM M2 keyboard is known for failing like this due to bad caps.  I opened up the keyboard and the caps were in very bad shape. I replaced the 47uF cap but needed to order a 2.2uF cap.  Hopefully that will restore the keyboard to a working state.

Added new 2.2uF 50v cap to the keyboard control board and it is now working.  Got it reassembled and realized the D key wasn’t working.  After a few disassembly and reassemblies, I found there was a small object under the bottom foil layer right below the pads for the D key membrane.   I suspect it was just enough to mess it up when assembled but not enough when I was testing the membrane separately.  

All keys are now working and the machine has been configured to boot into the simplified 4 panel view.  The HD is still working and has DOS and Windows 3.0 installed, as well as MS Works.

notes.note: Finally opened it up and pulled the logic board.  Looks like it was already recapped.  I may still do it to at least put in OS-CON caps for the 47uF ones.  The fan wasn't plugged in, nor was the optical drive.
Swapped ZuluSCSI for BlueSCSI with same image and it is MUCH faster.  Computer is usable now.  Will have to see if it is an SD card problem, or just an issue with this ZuluSCSI.

Added a front mounted BlueSCSI in the place of the MO drive so I could access the SD card without opening it up. 

I did swap the CPU for a 68040 I had from a damaged machine, but got some weird behavior so I put the engineering sample back in.  Had some weird boot issues that were resolved by going into the ROM monitor and setting the boot device to bsd(0,0,0) it’s back to working well.  

I also confirmed that with a clean install of the disk image onto the SD card, the zuluSCSI performed just fine. 

Need to recap the MO drive and the power supply.
